What it is: Primary fluid-shear dose metric: the spatial rate of change of water velocity a fish is exposed to.
How it is calculated: Mean velocity gradient dU/dy across the shear layer; in the PNNL submerged-jet apparatus computed from nozzle exit velocity and jet geometry over a fish-relevant length scale.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Value depends on the length scale and where it is computed (point vs fish-body scale); NOT directly comparable across apparatus/geometries.

What it is: The controlled exposure variable in jet studies, from which strain rate is derived.
How it is calculated: Set nozzle/orifice exit velocity into still water (e.g. 6.35-cm jet, 3-22.9 m/s).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Velocity is not strain rate; geometry-specific; relative vs absolute velocity must be stated.

What it is: Force per unit area from the velocity gradient.
How it is calculated: tau = mu x (dU/dy), dynamic viscosity times velocity gradient.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: FREQUENTLY CONFLATED with strain rate in secondary literature; a different physical quantity.

What it is: Whole-body acceleration experienced in the shear field; an alternative dose predictor.
How it is calculated: Motion-tracking of high-speed video, or Sensor-Fish accelerometer.
Used by (examples): Deng 2005 (best predictor of eye/opercle injury); Sensor Fish
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Device/length-scale dependent; links lab and field but calibration to live-fish injury is limited.

What it is: The strain rate below which no injury is detected, and onset thresholds for minor/major/fatal injury.
How it is calculated: Lowest strain rate (or nozzle velocity) at which each injury class first appears.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Onset depends on species, size, orientation and injury definition; thresholds are apparatus-specific.

What it is: Probability of a given injury (eye, operculum, gill, bruising) vs dose.
How it is calculated: Logistic regression of injury vs strain rate / velocity / acceleration.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Injury taxonomies and which injuries are 'severe' differ between labs.

What it is: Turbulence (closely related to shear) as a behavioural and exposure stressor.
How it is calculated: Turbulence intensity or turbulent kinetic energy from velocity measurements/CFD.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Often discussed with shear but rarely linked quantitatively to injury.

What it is: Field exposure proxy: physical conditions an autonomous sensor experiences in passage.
How it is calculated: Sensor-Fish acceleration/pressure traces processed into exposure events.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Measures EXPOSURE, not live-fish injury; sensor != live fish response.
